title: Tectonic hazards — why the ground shakes and volcanoes erupt
examTip: AQA Geography Paper 1, Topic 1 (The Challenge of Natural Hazards). Expect 4–8 mark questions on plate boundary types, case study comparisons (HIC vs LIC responses), and the reasons people live near hazards. Case study questions are high-value — learn Japan 2011 and Haiti 2010 in detail.
introTips: Covers all four plate boundary types and the hazards each one produces
You'll be able to compare earthquake and volcano features and effects with precision
By the end you can evaluate why people choose to live near tectonic hazards — a common 6-mark question
intro: Every year, earthquakes and volcanic eruptions reshape landscapes and lives. Understanding why they happen — and why millions of people still live in hazard zones — is at the heart of AQA's Natural Hazards topic. This isn't just abstract geology: examiners want you to connect the physical processes to real human decisions and case study outcomes.

keyPoints.heading: The Earth's structure and what moves the plates
keyPoints.content: The Earth has four layers. The inner core is solid iron and nickel, reaching temperatures of around 5,000°C. Surrounding it is the outer core, which is liquid. Above that is the mantle — a thick layer of semi-molten rock called magma. The outermost layer is the thin, solid crust. The crust and upper mantle together form the lithosphere, broken into large sections called tectonic plates. There are two types: continental plates are thicker (30–50 km) and less dense, made of granite-type rock; oceanic plates are thinner (5–10 km) and denser, made of basalt. Plates move because of convection currents in the mantle — heat from the core causes magma to rise, spread, cool, and sink in a slow circular motion, dragging the plates with it. Plates move at roughly 2–5 cm per year.
writeBullets: Inner core — solid iron and nickel, ~5,000°C
Outer core — liquid iron and nickel
Mantle — semi-molten rock (magma), site of convection currents
Crust — thin outermost layer; continental (thicker, less dense) or oceanic (thinner, denser)
Convection currents — heat-driven circular movement in the mantle that drags plates

keyTerms.def: A large section of the Earth's lithosphere that moves slowly over the mantle
Circular movement of magma in the mantle driven by heat, which moves tectonic plates
Thicker, less dense crust made of granite-type rock
Thinner, denser crust made of basalt

keyPoints.memTip: For the layers: 'I Obviously Make Crust' — Inner core, Outer core, Mantle, Crust. Cover your notes and write all four layers with a key fact for each from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Many students mix up continental and oceanic — remember: oceanic = ocean floor = denser and thinner. When asked 'what drives plate movement?' always say convection currents AND explain the mechanism (heat rises, spreads, cools, sinks). One word is not enough for marks.

keyPoints.heading: The four types of plate boundary
keyPoints.content: Where plates meet, different movements create different hazards. At a destructive boundary, an oceanic plate is forced under a continental plate — this is called subduction. The oceanic plate melts in the mantle, and the rising magma creates composite volcanoes and powerful earthquakes. The Pacific Ring of Fire is the key example. At a constructive boundary, plates move apart and magma rises to fill the gap, forming new crust and shield volcanoes. Earthquakes occur but are less powerful. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is the classic example, where Iceland sits above it. At a conservative boundary, plates slide past each other horizontally — no crust is created or destroyed, so there are no volcanoes, but powerful earthquakes occur. The San Andreas Fault in California is the exam example. At a collision boundary, two continental plates collide — neither subducts because both are less dense, so they crumple upwards to form fold mountains. The Himalayas (India–Eurasian plates) are the key example. Earthquakes occur; no volcanoes.
writeBullets: Destructive — oceanic subducts under continental; composite volcanoes + powerful earthquakes; e.g. Pacific Ring of Fire
Constructive — plates move apart; shield volcanoes + gentle earthquakes; e.g. Mid-Atlantic Ridge / Iceland
Conservative — plates slide past; earthquakes only, no volcanoes; e.g. San Andreas Fault, California
Collision — two continental plates crumple upward; fold mountains + earthquakes; e.g. Himalayas
Subduction — when a denser oceanic plate is forced under a less dense continental plate

keyTerms.def: Where oceanic plate subducts under continental plate, producing volcanoes and powerful earthquakes
Where plates diverge and magma rises to form new crust and shield volcanoes
Where plates slide past each other, causing earthquakes but no volcanoes
Where two continental plates collide and fold upward into mountains
The sinking of a denser oceanic plate beneath a continental plate into the mantle

keyPoints.memTip: Remember DCCC: Destructive, Constructive, Conservative, Collision — each gets its own hazard pattern. Cover your notes and write the boundary type, movement, hazards, and example location for all four from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Conservative boundaries are the trickiest — students often say volcanoes form there. They don't. Plates slide past, nothing goes up or down. San Andreas is the must-know example. For 4+ mark questions always give the example location and the hazard type together.

keyPoints.heading: Earthquakes: how they work and their effects
keyPoints.content: Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates suddenly slip. The point underground where energy is released is called the focus (or hypocentre). Directly above it on the surface is the epicentre — the point that experiences the strongest shaking. Energy travels outward as seismic waves, recorded by a seismometer and measured on the Richter scale (logarithmic — each step is 10 times more powerful). Earthquakes produce primary effects — the immediate impacts of the shaking itself: building collapse, deaths, injured people, broken infrastructure. They also cause secondary effects — consequences that happen as a result of the primary effects: fires from broken gas pipes, tsunamis (if the focus is under the sea), landslides, disease from contaminated water. Responses are divided into immediate responses (search and rescue, emergency medical care, providing food/water in the first 48–72 hours) and long-term responses (rebuilding, improved planning, earthquake-proof buildings, early warning systems).
writeBullets: Focus — point underground where earthquake energy is released
Epicentre — point on the surface directly above the focus; strongest shaking
Seismic waves — energy waves that travel outward from the focus
Richter scale — logarithmic scale measuring earthquake magnitude
Primary effects — immediate: building collapse, deaths, broken roads
Secondary effects — fires, tsunamis, landslides, disease outbreaks

keyTerms.def: The underground point where earthquake energy is released
The point on Earth's surface directly above the focus
Instrument that detects and records seismic waves
Logarithmic scale measuring earthquake magnitude; each step is 10× more powerful
A large ocean wave triggered by an underwater earthquake displacing the sea floor

keyPoints.memTip: Focus is underground (like the 'focus' of a problem at its source); epicentre is on the surface (epi = above in Greek). Cover your notes and recall: focus definition, epicentre definition, primary vs secondary effects with two examples each.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Always separate primary and secondary effects clearly in exam answers — they are worth separate marks. A very common error is calling a tsunami a primary effect. It's secondary, because it's caused by the earthquake, not the shaking itself.

keyPoints.heading: Volcanoes: types, products, and effects
keyPoints.content: Not all volcanoes are the same — the type depends on the plate boundary. Composite volcanoes (also called stratovolcanoes) form at destructive boundaries. They have steep sides, alternate layers of lava and ash, and erupt explosively with thick, viscous lava. Examples include Mount Pinatubo and Mount St. Helens. Shield volcanoes form at constructive boundaries (and over hotspots). They have gentle slopes, runny basaltic lava that flows easily, and erupt frequently but less violently. Mauna Loa in Hawaii is the classic example. Volcanic products include: lava flows (slower, destroys property), ash clouds (disrupts air travel, damages crops, causes roof collapse), pyroclastic flows (fast-moving clouds of superheated gas, ash, and rock — highly deadly), and volcanic gases (e.g. sulfur dioxide, which can cause acid rain). Primary effects include deaths, destroyed buildings, and burning forests. Secondary effects include disruption to farming, lahars (volcanic mudflows), and economic losses from tourism decline.
writeBullets: Composite volcano — steep, explosive, thick lava, alternating ash/lava layers; destructive boundaries
Shield volcano — gentle slopes, runny lava, frequent but less violent; constructive boundaries/hotspots
Pyroclastic flow — superheated cloud of gas, ash and rock; moves at up to 700 km/h; most deadly product
Lahar — volcanic mudflow; secondary effect as ash mixes with water from rain or ice melt
Ash cloud — primary effect; disrupts aviation, collapses roofs, damages crops

keyTerms.def: Steep-sided volcano with alternating ash and lava layers; forms at destructive boundaries; erupts explosively
Broad, gently-sloping volcano with runny lava; forms at constructive boundaries and hotspots
Fast-moving current of hot gas, ash and rock fragments — extremely dangerous
A destructive mudflow of volcanic ash and water flowing down a volcano's flanks

keyPoints.memTip: Composite = complex and explosive (both start with C, and they're both dramatic). Shield = wide and gentle (a shield is flat). Cover your notes and compare both volcano types across: shape, lava type, eruption style, and boundary type.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Examiners love asking you to explain why a composite volcano is more dangerous than a shield volcano — your answer should mention the lava viscosity (thick = traps gas = explosive) and the pyroclastic flows, not just 'it explodes more'.

keyPoints.heading: Why people live near hazards — and two case studies
keyPoints.content:
Knowing the risk is not enough to make people leave. People live near tectonic hazards for several reasons: fertile soils from volcanic ash make surrounding farmland extremely productive (e.g. Java, Indonesia); geothermal energy from volcanic heat provides cheap electricity (e.g. Iceland); tourism generates income from visitors drawn to dramatic landscapes; poverty means people cannot afford to move, and long return periods between eruptions mean the risk feels remote.

For AQA, you need two contrasting earthquake case studies. Haiti 2010 (LIC): magnitude 7.0, 230,000 deaths, 1.5 million homeless. Poor infrastructure, corrupt government, minimal emergency services, and international aid arrived slowly. Long-term recovery was extremely slow. Japan 2011 (HIC): magnitude 9.0 (much larger), around 16,000 deaths (many from the tsunami, not the quake itself). Japan's strict building codes, early warning systems, tsunami walls, and well-rehearsed emergency plans meant far fewer deaths despite a much stronger quake. The contrast shows how level of development shapes vulnerability.
writeBullets: Fertile soils — volcanic ash weathers into rich farmland, attracting farming communities
Geothermal energy — volcanic heat used for electricity and heating (e.g. Iceland)
Tourism — dramatic scenery and hot springs bring economic benefit
Haiti 2010 — M7.0, 230,000 deaths, LIC: poor infrastructure, slow response, long recovery
Japan 2011 — M9.0, ~16,000 deaths (mainly tsunami), HIC: building codes, warnings, rehearsed plans
Development gap — wealthier countries prepare better and recover faster from the same scale of hazard

keyTerms.def: The average time between events of a given magnitude; long gaps make risk feel low
Energy harnessed from underground heat in volcanically active areas
How susceptible a population is to harm from a hazard, shaped by wealth, preparation, and governance

keyPoints.memTip: For case studies: Haiti = Heartbreaking outcome due to poverty (H for H). Japan = Just managed it due to preparation (J for J). Cover your notes and write five specific facts for each case study from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: For 6-mark 'evaluate' questions on case studies, you must use named evidence — death tolls, magnitudes, specific responses — not just 'Haiti was poor'. Examiners reward specificity. Learn at least five data points per case study.

tables.title: Comparison of plate boundary types
rows: Shield volcanoes, gentle earthquakes, new crust

0.question: Which type of plate boundary produces volcanoes but NO earthquakes? (Careful — this is a trick question.)
options: None — all plate boundaries that have volcanoes also have earthquakes
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. All volcano-producing boundaries (destructive and constructive) also produce earthquakes. There is no boundary type with volcanoes but no earthquakes.
0.wrongFeedback: This is a classic trick. Conservative boundaries have earthquakes but NO volcanoes. Collision boundaries have earthquakes but NO volcanoes. All boundary types that produce volcanoes (destructive and constructive) also produce earthquakes. The answer is 'None — all boundaries with volcanoes also have earthquakes'.

2.question: What is the difference between the focus and the epicentre of an earthquake?
options: The focus is on the surface; the epicentre is underground
The focus is underground where energy is released; the epicentre is on the surface directly above
They are two names for the same point
The epicentre is where the tsunami starts; the focus is where shaking begins
2.correctFeedback: Correct. The focus is the underground source of energy release. The epicentre is directly above it on the surface — and it experiences the strongest shaking.
2.wrongFeedback: It's the other way around. The focus is underground — that's the source of the earthquake energy. The epicentre is on the surface directly above the focus. Think: 'epi' = above (like epidemic spreading above the source).

4.question: Why did Haiti (2010, M7.0) suffer far more deaths than Japan (2011, M9.0) despite Japan's earthquake being much more powerful?
options: Haiti is closer to a plate boundary than Japan
Japan had a smaller population in the affected area
Japan's wealth meant better building codes, early warning systems, and emergency planning
Haiti had more composite volcanoes which amplified the damage
4.correctFeedback: Exactly. Development level is the key variable. Japan's strict earthquake-proof building codes, tsunami walls, early warning systems, and rehearsed evacuation plans saved many lives despite a far stronger quake.
4.wrongFeedback: The key factor is level of development. Japan is a HIC with strict building regulations, tsunami early warning systems, and well-rehearsed evacuation plans. Haiti is a LIC with poor infrastructure and limited emergency capacity. A stronger earthquake killed far fewer people in Japan — that gap is explained by preparation, not location.

commonMistakes: Saying volcanoes form at conservative boundaries — they don't. Conservative boundaries produce earthquakes only because plates slide past each other without any magma being created or released.
Confusing focus and epicentre — the focus is underground (the source of energy), the epicentre is on the surface above it.
Calling a tsunami a primary effect of an earthquake — it is secondary, because it results from the earthquake displacing the seafloor, not from the shaking itself.

Describing composite and shield volcanoes as the same type — composite volcanoes are steep and explosive (destructive boundaries), shield volcanoes are gentle-sloped and less explosive (constructive boundaries/hotspots).
Giving vague answers in case study questions — 'Haiti was poor' earns no marks on its own. Always use specific data: magnitude, death toll, named responses, named organisations.
examTips: For any 4+ mark question on plate boundaries, always name the boundary type, describe the movement, state the hazards produced, and give a real-world example location — all four earn marks.

Case study questions will ask you to 'compare' responses in an HIC and a LIC — structure your answer around specific named evidence (not vague generalities), and explicitly link the difference in impact to level of development.

When explaining why people live near tectonic hazards, give at least two distinct reasons (e.g. fertile soils AND poverty/lack of choice) and explain each one — a list without explanation won't score well.
The words 'primary' and 'secondary' effects must be used correctly in your answers — examiners look for them. Primary = immediate result of the hazard. Secondary = consequence of the primary effect.
For 'evaluate' questions, always reach a conclusion: which factor is most important? Which case study shows the greatest contrast? Unsupported answers stall at Level 2.
revisionCardBullets: Earth layers: inner core (solid), outer core (liquid), mantle (semi-molten), crust (solid)
Continental crust: thicker, less dense, granite; Oceanic crust: thinner, denser, basalt
Convection currents in the mantle drive plate movement at ~2–5 cm/year
Destructive: oceanic subducts under continental → composite volcanoes + powerful earthquakes (Ring of Fire)
Constructive: plates diverge → shield volcanoes + gentle earthquakes (Mid-Atlantic Ridge)
Conservative: plates slide past → earthquakes only, no volcanoes (San Andreas Fault)
Collision: two continental plates crumple → fold mountains + earthquakes (Himalayas)
Focus = underground energy source; epicentre = surface point above focus
Primary effects: immediate (building collapse, deaths); secondary effects: fires, tsunamis, disease
Composite volcanoes: steep, explosive, thick lava; Shield volcanoes: gentle slopes, runny lava
Haiti 2010 (LIC): M7.0, 230,000 deaths — poor infrastructure, slow response
Japan 2011 (HIC): M9.0, ~16,000 deaths — building codes, early warning, rehearsed evacuation
